items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: What does the 'I' in FITT stand for?
items.why: Students guess. FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type - the variables you adjust to apply progressive overload. Intensity = how hard (e.g., % of 1RM or heart rate).

items.q: Working at 85% of max HR is in which zone?
items.why: Correct answer: Anaerobic. “Resting” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does a faster recovery rate indicate?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Higher fitness. “Lower fitness” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is 70% of the max HR for a 30-year-old?
items.why: Exact answer: 133. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
